text_blocks.text: Production capacity in Britain and the United States
was improved by the invention of the cotton gin by the
American Eli Whitney in 1793. Before the development of
cotton gins, the cotton fibers had to be pulled from the
seeds tediously by hand. By the late 1700s a number of
crude ginning machines had been developed. However, to
produce a bale of cotton required over 600 hours of human
labor, making large-scale production uneconomical in the
United States, even with the use of humans as slave labor.
The gin that Whitney manufactured (the Holmes design)
reduced the hours down to just a dozen or so per bale.
Although Whitney patented his own design for a cotton gin,
he manufactured a prior design from Henry Odgen Holmes, for
which Holmes filed a patent in 1796. Improving technology and
increasing control of world markets allowed British traders
to develop a commercial chain in which raw cotton fibers
were (at first) purchased from colonial plantations, processed
into cotton cloth in the mills of Lancashire, and then
exported on British ships to captive colonial markets in
West Africa, India, and China (via Shanghai and Hong Kong).
